app_mention.description: Fires when the bot is @-mentioned in a channel message. The primary entry-point trigger for most Slack agents. Works for both top-level mentions and mentions inside existing threads — the thread_id ref coalesces event.thread_ts and event.ts so continuation works across both cases.

message.channels.description: Fires on every new message in a public channel the bot is in. Usually used with conditions that filter to specific patterns (mentions, thread replies to existing agent conversations) — without conditions this is noisy. Thread replies expose event.thread_ts; top-level messages have only event.ts.

message.groups.description: Fires on every new message in a private channel (group) the bot is in. Shape is identical to message.channels with channel_type = 'group'. Same noisy-without-conditions caveat.

message.im.description: Fires on a message sent to the bot in a direct message conversation (channel_type = 'im'). Each IM conversation is its own thread. Safe to listen without conditions — only messages addressed to the bot arrive here.

reaction_added.description: Fires when a user adds an emoji reaction to a message. The item object names the message that was reacted to; event.reaction is the emoji name. Useful for agents that respond to approval/disapproval reactions on their own output.

reaction_removed.description: Fires when a user removes an emoji reaction from a message. Same shape as reaction_added. Typically listened for alongside reaction_added to track reaction state on agent messages.

member_joined_channel.description: Fires when a user joins a channel the bot is in. Useful for welcome-flow agents. The inviter field is present when the user was added by another member; it's absent when the user self-joined a public channel.

member_left_channel.description: Fires when a user leaves a channel the bot is in. Same shape as member_joined_channel without the inviter field.

channel_created.description: Fires when a new channel is created in the workspace. The event.channel object contains the new channel's metadata (id, name, creator). Useful for governance agents that enforce naming conventions or auto-archive policies.

team_join.description: Fires when a new user joins the workspace. The event.user object contains the new member's profile information. Useful for onboarding agents that DM new members with welcome content.

token.description: Verification token — deprecated for most uses; signature verification is the correct approach

api_app_id.description: The app that is receiving this event (A...)

event.description: The event payload. Fields include type (always 'app_mention'), user (who mentioned), text, ts, channel, event_ts, and optionally thread_ts if the mention is inside an existing thread

event_time.description: Unix timestamp of when the event fired

authorizations.description: List of installations this event is authorized for

event.description: The message event. Includes type ('message'), channel, user, text, ts, event_ts, channel_type (channel|group|im|mpim), and optionally thread_ts (if in a thread) and subtype (file_share, message_changed, message_deleted, etc.)

The reaction event. Includes type (reaction_added|reaction_removed), user (who reacted), reaction (emoji name), item (object with type, channel, ts identifying the reacted-to message), item_user (the message author), and event_ts
The membership event. Includes type (member_joined_channel|member_left_channel), user, channel, channel_type (C|G|im|mpim), team, and optionally inviter (for joins added by another member)

The channel lifecycle event. Includes type (channel_created|channel_archive|channel_unarchive|channel_rename|channel_deleted) and a nested channel object with id, name, creator, created (unix timestamp), is_private, and other channel metadata
The team join event. Includes type ('team_join') and a nested user object with id, name, real_name, profile (email, display_name, title, image_*), team_id, and other user metadata
